intro_band.agent_remit_summary: The AutoGen code-executor family is the execution tier of a generate/execute pattern: it takes fenced Python or shell code blocks that an LLM agent produced and runs them inside a controlled, isolated environment, returning stdout, stderr, and exit status to the caller. The remit authorizes five executors — `LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor` (host, restricted), `DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor` (the recommended production path), `JupyterCodeExecutor` and `DockerJupyterCodeExecutor` (stateful kernels), and `ACADynamicSessionsCodeExecutor` (Azure cloud sandbox) — with `create_default_code_executor` preferring Docker. Execution must be confined to a configured working directory, bounded by a wall-clock timeout, forbidden from touching the parent process's environment or credentials, and forbidden from network egress beyond a configured allow-list; running on the local host in production requires operator approval.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: Python library under `autogen_ext.code_executors` (plus the `autogen_core.code_executor` abstraction). Each executor subclasses the `CodeExecutor` ABC in `_base.py` and implements `execute_code_blocks`, which writes each block to a file and runs it. `create_default_code_executor` in `__init__.py` picks Docker if the daemon answers, else silently falls back to the host executor on a warning. `LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor` runs blocks as host subprocesses with `env=os.environ.copy()`; `DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor` runs them in an ephemeral container via `containers.create`; `DockerJupyterServer` builds a kernel-gateway container and publishes its port; `JupyterCodeExecutor` runs an in-process nbclient kernel on the local host; `ACADynamicSessionsCodeExecutor` posts code to an authenticated Azure HTTPS endpoint. There is no approval hook and no structured execution audit log at the executor layer.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is isolation whose defaults quietly degrade it. The two strongest sandboxes (Docker, Azure) are real, but the paths around them fail open: create_default_code_executor silently downgrades to unisolated host execution on a mere warning when Docker is absent, and LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor hands LLM-generated code the parent process's full environment while its own docstring advertises a dangerous-command sanitizer that does not exist in the code. Even the recommended DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor ships with unrestricted network egress, no resource limits, and a root, un-hardened container by default — so the container that is meant to be the safety boundary is porous out of the box. Across all five executors there is no execution audit record and no detection of sandbox-escape attempts, leaving the divergences both exploitable and unobservable.

rules.rule_text: Confine all file reads and writes to a configured working directory (for the Azure executor, `/mnt/data`).
Enforce a configured wall-clock timeout on every execution and terminate any process that exceeds it.
When configured with an approval function, obtain approval for each code block before executing it.
Surface all errors (syntax, runtime, timeout, out-of-memory) back to the caller in structured form without silently discarding them.
Fetching its own dependencies at runtime from package registries without explicit configuration, or modifying its own code.
The executor must not initiate its own network traffic beyond a configured allow-list.
Any mechanism that loads or executes code fetched from a remote URL or other unverified source on the LLM's behalf.
Reading or modifying the parent process's environment, credentials, or state from within an execution.
Moving files or data outside the configured working directory.
Exfiltrating data over network egress not permitted by configuration.
Executing code on the local host executor in a production deployment (default production deployments must use a containerized executor).
Enabling any network egress from the execution environment beyond a configured allow-list.
Mounting host volumes into a container executor at any path other than the configured working directory.
Bypassing working-directory confinement by any means — symlinks, parent-directory traversal, absolute paths, or runtime-supplied volume-mount overrides.
Silently swallowing or transforming error output before returning it to the caller.
Executing code with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option achieves the same task.
Every execution is recorded with timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, working directory, timeout, exit status, and a digest (not the body) of the executed code.
A code execution attempts to read or write outside the working directory, or to escape the container/sandbox.
Embedded instructions in the code source attempt to subvert the sandbox (detected but blocked).

findings.summary: create_default_code_executor silently falls back to unisolated host execution when Docker is unavailable, defeating the containerized-default requirement.
findings.description: The factory prefers Docker but, when the daemon is absent or the container fails to initialize, drops to `LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor` behind a `warnings.warn` with no hard error and no approval gate. LLM-generated code then runs directly on the host with no working-directory confinement or isolation. The remit requires production to use a containerized executor and to gate host execution behind operator approval — this default fails open.
policy_rule_text: Executing code on the local host executor in a production deployment (default production deployments must use a containerized executor).
Executing code with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option achieves the same task.

evidence.snippet: _is_docker_available() gate at :58; on failure control falls through to warnings.warn (:69-75) then returns LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor (:77-80) — a warning, not a halt or approval prompt.
_execute_code_dont_check_setup runs each block as a host subprocess (cwd=work_dir) with no confinement of what the code may read or write on the host filesystem.

recommended_actions: Make the Docker-unavailable path raise by default instead of returning a host executor; require an explicit opt-in argument (e.g. allow_local=True) before `create_default_code_executor` will hand back `LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor`.
Route any host-executor selection through the operator approval mechanism the remit requires for production, and log the downgrade as an alertable event.

findings.summary: The local executor passes the full parent-process environment into LLM-generated code, exposing host credentials and secrets.
findings.description: `LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor` builds the subprocess environment with `env = os.environ.copy()` and passes it unchanged to the executed block. Any host credential, API key, or secret in the parent process's environment is readable — and exfiltratable — by the code the LLM generated. The remit forbids reading the parent process's environment or credentials from within an execution.
policy_rule_text: Reading or modifying the parent process's environment, credentials, or state from within an execution.

evidence.snippet: env = os.environ.copy() — full parent environment copied, then passed as env= to asyncio.create_subprocess_exec at :433 with no scrubbing of credential-bearing keys.

recommended_actions: Construct the subprocess environment from an allow-list (PATH plus explicitly configured variables) rather than copying `os.environ`, so host secrets are never inherited by executed code.

findings.summary: The local executor's docstring advertises regex sanitization of dangerous commands, but no such screening exists anywhere in the class.
findings.description: The class docstring states that command-line code is sanitized by regex against a list of dangerous commands to prevent self-destructive commands, yet `_execute_code_dont_check_setup` writes each block straight to a file and executes it with no denylist, regex, or screening of any kind. Operators reading the docstring may believe a guardrail protects them when none runs — a documented-but-absent control. The remit assumes embedded sandbox-subversion attempts are detected and blocked.
policy_rule_text: Embedded instructions in the code source attempt to subvert the sandbox (detected but blocked).

evidence.snippet: Docstring claims "Command line code is sanitized using regular expression match against a list of dangerous commands" — no matching sanitize/denylist function exists (grep for saniti/dangerous across all executors returns only this docstring).
_execute_code_dont_check_setup applies only silence_pip and a language check, then writes and runs the code verbatim — no dangerous-command screening on the path the docstring describes.

recommended_actions: Either implement the advertised screening (and document its exact, bypass-aware limits) or delete the sanitization claim from the docstring so operators do not rely on a control that does not exist.

findings.summary: The recommended Docker executor gives executed code unrestricted network egress by default, providing an exfiltration channel out of the sandbox.
findings.description: `containers.create` is called with no `network_mode`, so the container joins Docker's default bridge network with full outbound internet access. LLM-generated code running in the recommended production executor can therefore reach any external host and exfiltrate working-directory data with no allow-list. The remit forbids the executor from initiating network traffic beyond a configured allow-list and requires approval before egress is enabled.
policy_rule_text: The executor must not initiate its own network traffic beyond a configured allow-list.
Exfiltrating data over network egress not permitted by configuration.
Enabling any network egress from the execution environment beyond a configured allow-list.

evidence.snippet: containers.create(...) call, lines 537-550 — sets volumes/working_dir/extra_hosts/device_requests but no network_mode, leaving the container on the default bridge with outbound egress enabled.

recommended_actions: Default the container to `network_mode="none"` and require an explicit, operator-approved allow-list argument before any egress is permitted; document that enabling network access is a gated action.

findings.summary: The Docker executor creates its container with no resource limits, non-root user, or capability hardening, weakening the isolation boundary by default.
findings.description: The `containers.create` call sets no `mem_limit`, `cpu_quota`, or `pids_limit`, and no `user`, `cap_drop`, `security_opt=["no-new-privileges"]`, or `read_only` root filesystem. Executed code therefore runs as root inside the container with host-unbounded CPU/memory/PIDs, so a fork bomb or runaway process can exhaust the host and any container-escape primitive runs with maximal in-container privilege. The container that is meant to be the safety boundary ships un-hardened.

evidence.snippet: containers.create(...) lines 537-550 — no mem_limit/cpu/pids_limit, no user=, no cap_drop, no security_opt, no read_only; container runs as root with default (unlimited) resources.

recommended_actions: Add safe container defaults — a non-root `user`, `mem_limit`/`pids_limit`/CPU caps, `cap_drop=["ALL"]`, and `security_opt=["no-new-privileges"]` — exposing them as configurable ceilings that require approval to raise.

findings.summary: The Docker-Jupyter server publishes its code-executing kernel gateway on all host interfaces, and allows an empty (no-auth) token.
findings.description: The bundled Dockerfile runs the kernel gateway bound to `0.0.0.0`, and the server starts the container with `publish_all_ports=True`, so the gateway — a remote code-execution endpoint — is mapped to a host port reachable on every host interface, not just loopback. The default token is a random hex string (good), but the API documents an empty token for no authentication, which would expose an unauthenticated RCE endpoint to the network.

evidence.snippet: DEFAULT_DOCKERFILE runs kernelgateway --KernelGatewayApp.ip=0.0.0.0 (:278); container started with publish_all_ports=True (:368), mapping 8888 to a host port on all interfaces.
token param docstring "Empty for no auth" (:311) — an empty token disables gateway authentication, leaving the exposed RCE endpoint open.

recommended_actions: Bind the published port to 127.0.0.1 (or drop `publish_all_ports=True` in favor of an explicit loopback port map) and reject an empty token so the gateway cannot be started unauthenticated.

findings.summary: The Docker-Jupyter server makes the host bind directory world-writable (0o777), letting any local user tamper with executed code and outputs.
findings.description: When a `bind_dir` is supplied, the server calls `os.chmod(bind_dir, 0o777)` on the host directory that is mounted into the container as the working directory. Any local user on the host can then read, modify, or plant files that the kernel will execute or return, and can read outputs written there. The permission is broader than the executor's task requires and is set unconditionally.

evidence.snippet: os.chmod(bind_dir, 0o777) — the host-side bind directory is made world read/write/execute for every local account.

recommended_actions: Set the bind directory to owner-only permissions (e.g. 0o700) or the minimum the container UID requires, rather than 0o777.

findings.summary: The bundled Jupyter Dockerfile builds from an unpinned, unverified base image, exposing the execution environment to supply-chain drift.
findings.description: `DEFAULT_DOCKERFILE` uses `FROM quay.io/jupyter/docker-stacks-foundation` with no tag or digest, so each build resolves to whatever `latest` points at, with no digest pin or signature verification. The Docker executor's default `python:3-slim` is likewise a floating tag pulled without integrity checks. A compromised or shifted upstream image would alter the environment in which all LLM code runs, with no way to detect the change.

evidence.snippet: DEFAULT_DOCKERFILE = "FROM quay.io/jupyter/docker-stacks-foundation" — no tag/digest; image built and run with no integrity verification.
image: str = "python:3-slim" default — a floating tag pulled via client.images.pull (:524) with no digest pin or signature check.

recommended_actions: Pin base images by digest (or an explicit immutable tag) and verify them before build/run; document the pinned versions so drift is detectable.

findings.summary: No executor emits a structured per-execution audit record, and none detect or alert on sandbox-escape attempts.
findings.description: The remit specifies a per-execution log capturing timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, working directory, timeout, exit status, and a code digest, plus halt/alert behavior when code tries to leave the working directory or escape the sandbox. None of the five executors implement either — only ad-hoc operational `logging` calls exist in the Docker lifecycle, with no execution audit record and no escape detection. Divergences from the remit are therefore neither recorded nor observable.
policy_rule_text: Every execution is recorded with timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, working directory, timeout, exit status, and a digest (not the body) of the executed code.
A code execution attempts to read or write outside the working directory, or to escape the container/sandbox.

evidence.snippet: Only ad-hoc logging.debug/info/error for container lifecycle (e.g. :483) — no structured audit record of executed blocks; local/jupyter/azure executors emit no execution log at all.
_execute_code_dont_check_setup runs and returns each block with no timestamp/kind/language/source/digest/exit audit entry and no detection of out-of-workdir or escape attempts.

recommended_actions: Emit a structured audit event per execution (kind, language, source, work_dir, timeout, exit status, code digest — not the body) to a durable, schema'd sink, and surface an alertable signal when a block attempts to leave the working directory.

findings.summary: The local Jupyter executor runs LLM code in-process on the host with no isolation and, unlike the local command-line executor, no runtime warning.
findings.description: `JupyterCodeExecutor` starts an nbclient kernel that executes code on the local machine — an unisolated host-execution path equivalent in risk to `LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor`, and it inherits the host environment through the kernel process. But it emits no construction-time `warnings.warn`; the only signal is a `.. danger::` note in the docstring, so a caller wiring it up programmatically gets no runtime indication that execution is unsandboxed. It is a host-execution capability the remit's restricted-tools list does not name.

evidence.snippet: ".. danger:: This will execute code on the local machine" (:51-53) is the only signal; __init__ (:139-160) emits no warnings.warn, unlike LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor which warns at construction (local/__init__.py:163).

recommended_actions: Emit a construction-time `warnings.warn` for `JupyterCodeExecutor` matching the local command-line executor, and name it in the remit's restricted host-execution tools so its use is gated in production.

positives.title: Azure dynamic-sessions managed sandbox
positives.description: `ACADynamicSessionsCodeExecutor` runs code in an Azure-managed cloud sandbox with a per-session identity, Bearer-token-authenticated HTTPS, `/mnt/data` confinement, and a static package allow-list — the strongest isolation of the five executors.

positives.title: Enforced timeout and ephemeral container in the Docker executor
positives.description: The Docker executor wraps every block in a `timeout` command and runs it in an auto-removed container isolated from the host, so a single block cannot run unbounded and the container does not persist after use.

positives.title: Filename-directive path-traversal guard
positives.description: `get_file_name_from_content` resolves any `# filename:` directive and rejects it via `Path.relative_to` if it falls outside the workspace, blocking traversal through the filename hint.

positives.title: Random kernel-gateway auth token by default
positives.description: `DockerJupyterServer` generates a random 32-byte hex authentication token for the Jupyter kernel gateway when none is supplied, so the default configuration is authenticated rather than open.

log_files.no_logs_note: No execution audit logs are produced or routed to a file in scope — the executors emit only ad-hoc operational `logging` calls (Docker lifecycle) with no FileHandler configured — and the absence of the remit's per-execution audit record is filed as PRAX-2026-07-17-009.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. Genuine isolation exists in two of the five executors (Docker process/filesystem isolation with enforced timeouts; the Azure managed sandbox with per-session identity and `/mnt/data` confinement), which keeps the framework off the floor. But the posture is undercut by defaults that degrade the boundary — silent host fallback, egress-on containers, host-environment inheritance, and a documented-but-absent sanitizer — and by the near-total absence of an execution audit trail or any adversarial-testing evidence in the scanned scope. The result is a library that can be run safely but does not default to safe, and cannot show what it ran.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The executors enforce a language allowlist in code — `SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES` plus `lang_to_cmd` reject unknown languages, and Jupyter/Azure are Python-only — but there is no restriction on what executed code may do, so domain is constrained only at the language gate.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Docker and Azure confine data to the working directory and do not expose host state, but `LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor` passes the full parent-process environment (`os.environ.copy()`) into executed code, and stdout/stderr are returned to the caller unlabeled as untrusted output.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Real container/cloud isolation is operative on the recommended paths, but the defaults defeat zero trust — `create_default_code_executor` fails open to host execution, the Docker container ships with egress on and no privilege/resource hardening, the local executor claims a sanitizer it lacks, and no executor interposes an approval check on code before it runs.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Images are named but unpinned and unverified — the Docker default `python:3-slim` is a floating tag and the bundled Jupyter Dockerfile uses an untagged `quay.io/jupyter/docker-stacks-foundation` base pulled with no digest or signature check.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No adversarial-testing artifacts, injection fixtures, or evidence that testing drove design changes are present in the scanned executor scope (repository test suites, if any, lie outside the audited paths).

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: No executor emits a structured per-execution audit record (executor kind, language, source, code digest, exit status); only ad-hoc operational `logging` calls exist in the Docker lifecycle, and none of the remit's halt/alert-on-escape detection behaviors are implemented.
